content.text: The model is the reasoning engine at the centre of every agent. Choosing the wrong model for a task either caps the agent's capability (an underpowered model on a complex problem) or wastes significant cost and latency (an overpowered model on a trivial one). Model selection is not a one-time decision — in a multi-step or multi-agent system, different steps may benefit from different models, and the right choice at each step compounds into significant differences in total cost and quality.

attrs.alt: Model selection decision framework — three tiers by capability, latency, and cost

content.text: The Claude model family spans three capability tiers. Each exists to serve a different point in the cost-capability-latency trade-off space:

Sonnet 4.6 is the right default for a new agent. It handles most coding, analysis, planning, and research tasks well. Upgrade to Opus when quality is demonstrably insufficient; downgrade to Haiku for high-volume, simple subtasks where you have measured that quality is acceptable.
The model is passed as a string literal to
. Always use the full model identifier — never a short alias or a computed string from untrusted input:

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Default for most agent tasks: balanced capability and cost
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=4096,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Analyse this codebase and suggest refactors."}],
)

# Fast, cheap model for high-volume, simple subtasks
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    max_tokens=256,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Is this log line an error? Answer yes or no."}],
)

# Most capable model for hard reasoning
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-8",
    max_tokens=8192,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Design the security architecture for this system."}],
)
. The API rejects unknown aliases and the full ID is future-proof.
Commit the model string to source control
— if the model changes, it should be a deliberate code change, not an environment variable or user input.
— Haiku is wasted on 8192-token responses; Haiku's typical ceiling is 512. Matching
to the tier avoids paying for unused output budget.

When an agent handles a variety of task types in the same loop, a classification step selects the appropriate model before each call. A simple heuristic classifier based on keyword signals works for many pipelines; a meta-model call to a fast model can classify more accurately for complex routing:

from enum import Enum
import anthropic


class TaskClass(Enum):
    SIMPLE = "simple"       # lookup, classification, short extraction
    STANDARD = "standard"   # coding, analysis, planning, research
    COMPLEX = "complex"     # hard reasoning, novel architecture, long docs


MODEL_FOR_CLASS = {
    TaskClass.SIMPLE: "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    TaskClass.STANDARD: "claude-sonnet-4-6",
    TaskClass.COMPLEX: "claude-opus-4-8",
}

MAX_TOKENS_FOR_CLASS = {
    TaskClass.SIMPLE: 512,
    TaskClass.STANDARD: 4096,
    TaskClass.COMPLEX: 8192,
}


def classify_task(task: str) -> TaskClass:
    """
    Heuristic classifier — replace with domain knowledge or a fast
    meta-model call for production use.
    """
    task_lower = task.lower()
    simple_signals = ["yes or no", "classify", "extract", "is this", "what is the", "list the"]
    complex_signals = ["architecture", "security", "design", "novel", "trade-off", "proof"]

    if any(s in task_lower for s in simple_signals):
        return TaskClass.SIMPLE
    if any(s in task_lower for s in complex_signals):
        return TaskClass.COMPLEX
    return TaskClass.STANDARD


def run_task(task: str, tools: list[dict] | None = None) -> str:
    """Select model based on task classification and run the agent step."""
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    cls = classify_task(task)
    model = MODEL_FOR_CLASS[cls]
    max_tokens = MAX_TOKENS_FOR_CLASS[cls]

    kwargs = {
        "model": model,
        "max_tokens": max_tokens,
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": task}],
    }
    if tools:
        kwargs["tools"] = tools

    response = client.messages.create(**kwargs)
    for block in response.content:
        if hasattr(block, "text"):
            return block.text
    return ""
is a single dict to audit and update when new models are released.
— misclassifying a hard task as simple produces low-quality output. It is safer to over-provision than to under-provision.
— run representative tasks through Haiku and Sonnet and compare output quality. Only route to Haiku if the quality is genuinely acceptable for your use case.

In an orchestrator-worker architecture, the orchestrator that plans and decomposes tasks benefits from the most capable model, while the workers that execute individual tool-use steps can use a cheaper, faster model. This structure captures most of the quality benefit of Opus at a fraction of the cost:
— the hard reasoning (decomposition, synthesis) goes to Opus; the mechanical execution (file reads, API calls, code edits) goes to Sonnet.
— each worker starts with a fresh messages list for its subtask. There is no shared context between workers; only the orchestrator holds the full picture.
— keep synthesis prompts short. The orchestrator only needs the result summaries, not the full worker conversation histories.
In mixed-model pipelines, understanding where tokens are spent helps you tune the routing policy. Track usage per model and report it at the end of each agent run:

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# WRONG — model chosen based on untrusted external input
# def run(task: str, user_model_pref: str) -> str:
#     return client.messages.create(model=user_model_pref, ...)

# RIGHT — model is always a literal string or from a closed set
ALLOWED_MODELS = {
    "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    "claude-sonnet-4-6",
    "claude-opus-4-8",
}

def safe_run(task: str, preferred_model: str = "claude-sonnet-4-6") -> str:
    model = preferred_model if preferred_model in ALLOWED_MODELS else "claude-sonnet-4-6"
    response = client.messages.create(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=4096,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": task}],
    )
    for block in response.content:
        if hasattr(block, "text"):
            return block.text
    return ""


# WRONG — using Opus for every step of a high-volume pipeline
# This can cost 10-20x more than Sonnet for the same throughput

# RIGHT — benchmark your task class first, then choose
# Sonnet handles most coding/analysis tasks at 1/5 the Opus cost.
# Only upgrade when Sonnet's output quality is demonstrably insufficient.
Never accept a model name from untrusted input
— validate against a closed set of allowed models. An attacker who controls the model string can direct calls to unintended endpoints.
Do not default to Opus everywhere
— running every agent step through Opus costs 10–20× more than Sonnet for comparable results on standard tasks. Benchmark first.
Do not default to Haiku everywhere
— the cost savings are real, but quality degradation on complex tasks produces incorrect outputs that cost more to fix than they saved.
— capability gaps between tiers change with each model generation. A task that required Opus in one generation may run well on Sonnet in the next.
Context Window and Output Token Limits
All Claude models in the current generation share a 200 000-token input context window. Output token limits differ by model and matter for
higher than needed adds no quality benefit but increases latency — the API buffers until the stop reason fires:

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Model context windows (input tokens):
#   claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 : 200 000 tokens
#   claude-sonnet-4-6         : 200 000 tokens
#   claude-opus-4-8           : 200 000 tokens
#
# Max output tokens per model call:
#   claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 : 8 192
#   claude-sonnet-4-6         : 8 192  (extended thinking: up to 64 000)
#   claude-opus-4-8           : 8 192  (extended thinking: up to 32 000)
#
# Rule: set max_tokens to what the task actually needs.
# Over-requesting wastes latency on empty output buffering.

def select_max_tokens(task_class: str, expect_long_output: bool = False) -> int:
    """
    Return a sensible max_tokens value based on expected output length.
    These are guidelines, not hard limits — adjust based on measurement.
    """
    base = {
        "simple": 512,
        "standard": 4096,
        "complex": 8192,
    }.get(task_class, 4096)

    if expect_long_output:
        return min(base * 2, 8192)
    return base
All three models support 200k input context
— context window size is not a model selection criterion in the current generation.
Output limits are 8 192 tokens by default
— for longer outputs, extended thinking mode raises the ceiling on Sonnet and Opus, but adds significant latency and cost.
— a classification call that returns "yes" or "no" wastes time buffering with

High-throughput agents may exhaust per-model rate limits, especially on Sonnet during peak periods. A simple fallback pattern tries the preferred model and falls back to a cheaper alternative on rate-limit errors:

Fallback to a less capable model only when the task class permits it. Never silently fall back from Opus to Haiku on a complex reasoning task — the output quality drop will be noticeable and may produce incorrect results.

The only reliable way to know whether a cheaper model is good enough for a task is to measure it. Run the same representative prompts through each candidate model and compare latency, token cost, and output quality before committing to a routing policy:

import anthropic
import time


def benchmark_model_for_task(
    task: str,
    models: list[str],
    runs: int = 3,
) -> dict[str, dict]:
    """
    Run the same task through multiple models and record latency, token usage,
    and output. Use this to decide which model tier is appropriate.
    """
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    results = {}

    for model in models:
        latencies = []
        token_counts = []
        outputs = []

        for _ in range(runs):
            t0 = time.monotonic()
            response = client.messages.create(
                model=model,
                max_tokens=1024,
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": task}],
            )
            latency = time.monotonic() - t0
            latencies.append(latency)
            token_counts.append(
                response.usage.input_tokens + response.usage.output_tokens
            )
            for block in response.content:
                if hasattr(block, "text"):
                    outputs.append(block.text)
                    break

        results[model] = {
            "avg_latency_s": sum(latencies) / len(latencies),
            "avg_tokens": sum(token_counts) / len(token_counts),
            "sample_output": outputs[0] if outputs else "",
        }

    return results


# Example:
# results = benchmark_model_for_task(
#     task="Classify this log line as ERROR, WARN, or INFO: '[2024] Connection reset by peer'",
#     models=["claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", "claude-sonnet-4-6"],
# )
# for model, stats in results.items():
#     print(f"{model}: {stats['avg_latency_s']:.2f}s, {stats['avg_tokens']:.0f} tokens")
#     print(f"  Output: {stats['sample_output'][:100]}")
Use at least 3 runs per model
— latency varies; a single measurement is not representative.
— automated metrics (BLEU, exact match) miss semantic quality. Read the sample outputs and judge.
— the capability gap between tiers shifts with each generation. A task that needed Sonnet in the previous generation may be Haiku-quality in the next.

Model selection is a cost-capability-latency trade-off made at every agent call. Use Sonnet 4.6 as the default for most tasks; upgrade to Opus 4.8 when hard reasoning quality is demonstrably insufficient; route simple, high-volume subtasks to Haiku 4.5 when you have confirmed the quality is acceptable. In multi-agent architectures, let the orchestrator use the most capable model and keep workers on the balanced tier. Always pass model identifiers as string literals from a closed, validated set. Track token usage per model to understand where cost accumulates and where routing adjustments will have the most impact.
